story:
Five days had passed since I last saw Mom. Jenine Killian—forty-six, five foot three, pale brown skin, dark hair thick enough to hide a squirrel family. Always in Dad’s rust cardigan, three sizes too big, like a hand-me-down hug. Dad’s gone, left a crater. It’s hard to mourn a hole. The grief is a ghost with no ties, pacing the room, refusing the door. I promised not to cry in front of her anymore, but promises melt at three a.m. when you wake to an empty bed.

The last time, she was on the porch, chin tipped up, staring at the stars like chunks of meat floating in a murky stew. Her eyes swallowed them whole. I ran room to room like a rat on fire before I found her, wrapped my arms around her thin shoulders, inhaled tea tree and home. She didn’t look at me. I dragged her back to bed, her feet scraping our water-warped boards that still held us up above the dirt, as if that was their only job.

Then one morning she was gone for real. Her side of the bed warm, her dent the only thing left to hold. No bowl in the sink. No shoes by the door. The lock loose as a baby tooth, enough for anyone to walk in, or for Mom to walk out. Five days is long enough to know when the thread between you is still pulled tight and tugging you in one direction. I followed.

March rain had hosed away footprints. I pushed into the dark pines that hung like gallows, their tops lost to cloud. Mom had taught me about rings in trees—each year added, each layer a badge around a dark birthmark in the middle. I yelled “Mom!” until the word got smaller in my throat. Mom. Mom. mom.

The ground opened its mouth. I nearly fell into a raw hole clawed in the heart of the woods. Fifteen feet down, I saw gold: my mother, digging. Nails broken, muddy blood in the beds. Dirt flying behind her like a dog at the beach.

“Mom?”

She didn’t look up. The louder I yelled, the faster she tore at the earth. I fetched Dad’s old ladder; by the time I returned, she was deeper, eyes red as overfilled balloons I wanted to pop. The hole made its own silence, a bubble that kept the world out and trapped me with the stranger wearing my mother.

“Did I do something wrong?” I asked.

“Not…you…” Her voice crackled, a wire sparking in her throat. And she dug. Sun fell, night rose. She disappeared to the waist, then to the ankles, legs kicking like pale twigs. I grabbed one, got kicked off. Peekaboo in the tub flashed across my mind—how she’d vanish behind bubbles and always come back.

Dirt is not bubbles.

I crawled in after her. The earth gave way.

Cold. A meat-locker breeze. Shallow gasps and dry coughs stitched the air. Light dripped from other holes where solemn adults dropped in and joined the slow river. Bony limbs, hollowed faces, mud-slick feet. Children wove among them, the small ones reaching up for hands that didn’t reach back, the older ones with hands in pockets, searching without saying it out loud.

I called until my voice broke. No one turned. Time blurred. Finally, I found her near the wall, wearing that dinner-time blankness that used to set a third plate before memory corrected her. I had come to find Mom, not this husk that carried her history. But this is where grief walks—down a narrow tunnel, past and present scraping the ribs. She had said goodbye without words, raised me anyway, and somewhere the past swallowed her. The thread between us still pulled, but it couldn’t turn her around.

They say if you want to know the future of a child, look at their parent. Down there, I did.

We all know where we’re going in the end.
